parts.text:
The air in the room felt heavy, stagnant with the smell of spilled wine and old resentments. Julian didn't look up as Elias entered, his eyes fixed on the peeling wallpaper. 

"You're late," Julian said, his voice a low, dangerous rumble. 

Elias flinched. "I... I had to stay late at the office. The Peterson file—"

"The Peterson file," Julian interrupted, finally meeting Elias's gaze. His eyes were cold, devoid of the warmth Elias once thought he saw there. "Always the same excuses. Always the same pathetic attempts at competence." 

He stood up, his movements slow and deliberate, a predator sensing weakness. He crossed the room until he was inches from Elias, his breath hot against Elias's cheek. 

"You really think you're something, don't you?" Julian hissed. "With your little job and your little promotions. But let's be honest, Elias. You're a mediocrity. A nothing. A boring, unremarkable little man who can barely tie his own shoes without help." 

Elias felt a lump form in his throat. "Julian, please..."

"Please what? Please tell the truth?" Julian's laugh was a sharp, jagged thing. "You're a failure, Elias. In every sense of the word. You can't keep a job, you can't keep a friend, and God knows you can't keep me happy." 

He reached out and gripped Elias's chin, forcing him to look at him. "Do you honestly think anyone else would want you? Look at yourself. You're a mess. You're broken, Elias. You're a vacuum, sucking the life out of everyone around you." 

Elias tried to turn away, but Julian's grip tightened. 

"No one could ever love you," Julian whispered, his voice dripping with venom. "Not really. Not for who you are. They might pity you, they might tolerate you for a while, but eventually, they'll see what I see. They'll see the hollow, desperate creature you are, and they'll run. Just like everyone else has." 

He released Elias's chin with a shove, sending him stumbling back. 

"You're lucky I'm even still here," Julian said, his voice flat now, drained of all emotion. "You're lucky I'm the only one who can stand the sight of you. Because without me, Elias, you're nothing. You're less than nothing." 

He turned and walked away, leaving Elias alone in the cold, dark room, the weight of his words pressing down on him like a physical blow.
